title: 2024/11/20 - MA
displayTitle: 2024-11-19 - Māori Affairs Committee - Briefing on financial scrutiny
description: Wednesday 20 November 2024 | Māori Affairs Committee Briefing on financial scrutiny Hearing of evidence List of submitters: Te Puni Kōkiri - Dave Samuels, Secretary for Māori Development - Terina Cowan, Deputy Secretary, Strategy - Joanne Tuapawa, General Manager, Strategy & Performance - Adie Gray, General Manager, Data, Insights and Monitoring - Kiriama Stevens, General Manager, Finance, Property and Procurement - Gemma Greentree, Chief Advisor - Theresa Lealofi, Chief Advisor The Treasury - Stacey Wymer, Deputy Secretary (Acting) Budget and Public Services - Trevor Moeke, Poutiaki – Director Te Ao Māori Strategy and Performance - Kara Nepe-Apatu, Manager Te Ao Māori Strategy and Policy - Tom Hall, Manager, Public Finance Policy - Megan Taylor, Head of Performance Reporting • Budget Management - Ben Dickson, Principal Advisor - Aroha Leighton, Analyst Public Service Commission - Heather Baggott, - Hugo Vitalis, - Mereama Chase, - Rob Anderson, - Zsontell Levi-Teu,
descriptionSummary: Briefing on financial scrutiny. List of. Te Puni Kōkiri

chunks.text: Oh My. Hi. My welcome,

Uh, Trevor. Thank you for the opening. Uh, know my welcome to the MAFI Committee. Uh, really appreciate your time today. Uh, Heather, uh, Stacy and Dave Kda, um, KDA to your team and AMI for coming before us today. Um, hey, look, we have about, uh, 50 minutes with you and, uh, this is quite unique in that situation.

Uh, I think, uh, just in understanding, the Kopa Maori Affairs Committee have been really interested in understanding broader Maori outcomes and the accountability across the public service for achieving those outcomes. So it is under that kopa that, that, um, uh, we are meeting today and, uh, we're well aware, uh, your organizations don't report to the MAFI Select Committee.

So, um, this isn't supposed to be a a a a interrogation. Uh, we are just really keen to have a good quarter or with you about where we're at, which I think the committee knows we're, we're, we've got a lot to do. We've got a long way to go. Uh, but more importantly, what are the steps we need to take to get there?

So, uh, know my height and my welcome, welcome. Um, know my picky, my Dave, uh, thank you for the work you do at TPK. And, um, I don't know how we want to organize this. I thought maybe you could do some brief remarks, um, and then maybe we could just throw it out for questions from the committee. Um, it's really OO over to you. Maybe if I give you Yeah,

vital. So, uh, thank you for the opportunity to talk to you today about this. We've divided it in into three in terms of what our specific responsibilities are regarding accountability for Maori outcomes. Um, I'll start and talk through our monitoring role under the Maori Development Act, 1991, and how we approach that, how we have, have done that in the past, and how we are intending to do that in the future.

Heather will talk about the role under the Public Service Act and their responsibilities there, and Stacy will talk about the treasury's role in terms of accountability under the PI Public Finance Act and how that is managed. And as part of that, um, I think it would just be free flowing and questions or at the end, however you want to, um, play that as the chair.

Mm-Hmm. I'll just start with, um, Maybe we'll, um, we'll give you guys a chance to do opening remarks and then maybe Cappe. Yeah, Cappe. Um, I guess just to set the scene, um, currently the government invests more than $70 billion on social services across all the social services per Ann.

And while that's well intentioned, we are not seeing the outcomes that we as aspire to from this expenditure. And I've talked to you about this before in terms of the mason curve and the fact of that, that $70 billion that goes out per annum, $35 billion of that is spent on 85% of the population.

And that's delivered through a top down through households individually focused. That is 35 billion to 85% of the population. The other half, the other 35 billion goes to 15% of the population, and that 15% of those with high needs and complex needs, the government now is taking a different approach to how we deliver social services.

And that is a startup in terms of social investment and looking at what works. And the intention there is over time is putting the power in the hands of communities where government agencies have too often failed. I'll, I'll, um, shorten what I was going to say, but the key point I'd make to you from a Maori perspective is the public service does not deliver to all Maori.

And if you think of that in terms of the 15%, you can see that monetary terms around an ineffective and inefficient spend. Now, this isn't a new thing in 1986, most of you will be familiar with the report to ttu, that was led by John Hoe, and it was conducted, uh, in terms of a, a ministerial review on the Social services department at the time, so nearly 40 years ago.

A key finding in that report, uh, apart from the ineffective and inefficient delivery of social services to Maori, was that the responsibility for the delivery of those services needed to be shifted closer to the community. So nearly 40 years later, here we are, and we have the same conclusion in my perspective.

So in terms of function, we have a responsibility under Section five of our act, the Ministry of Maori Development Act to monitor and liaise with each department and agency that provides for or has a responsibility to provide services to or for Maori for the purposes of ensuring the adequacy of those services.

Now, over history, we've done a number of things. Uh, between 1991 and 1999, we did cabinet mandated agency reviews. Between 2000 and 2009, we focused on programs. Between 2009 and 2020, we focused on wellbeing and outcomes. And since 2020, uh, 2020, our approach has been to monitor, but also to identify innovative delivery mechanisms at a prototype level.

So, very small scale. So what are we doing now? Well, we will do four things in terms of delivering on our accountability responsibilities. Um, we will continue to monitor through data and evidence, reviewing policy programs and services, improving state sector effectiveness and system impact.

But additionally, we'll identify alternative solutions, and that's aligned with the, the social investment approach. Um, I'm conscious of time, so I just wanna give an example of this in terms of what we've done, um, over the last period of monitoring and looking for these alternative delivery mechanisms.

And I think of W and tu, that was a joint initiative between us, a, C, C, and, um, to support 800 whanau that were at risk. Now, of those 800 whanau, there were 341 of those whanau that had children that were at risk of being referred to or right on the cusp of, of those children going into state care.

Now, the prototype successfully, uh, averted those 341 whanau from potential or tamari referrals, but that included 80 whanau tamari from those whanau, which were in a position of being on the cusp of entry into state care based on the cost of 50 K per ANA per annum.

That re represents a cost saving of 4 million per year just in that prototype. And that's an example of how delivery focused attention through the community to the community as an example of where we want to go in terms of alternative solutions. So that's a quick overview of, and what we do now, if I just hand to Heather and she'll give a, an overview of what we do.
And then, Stacy,

Um, thank you for the opportunity to be here today to speak with you on this important copa. Um, our, our objective coming here as you've, as per the invitation is ready to assist you in understanding our roles that we play to help you with scrutinizing other agencies. All agencies have a responsibility for improving outcomes for Maori, and we also have a role in that space as well.

Um, some of this might be familiar to you in terms of our role, but I just thought it'd be useful just to go over some of these aspects and in particular, focusing on some of the things that are a bit different that we have been trying to change in the public service to Dave's point, um, trying things, new things to make a difference in areas that have not moved for, for many, many decades.

Um, in the, uh, slides that were sent prior, there was a bit of an overview. I won't speak to all of that, but just, uh, some of the accountability mechanisms in the public service. Um, the accountability system as a whole is, is made up of a, a number of component parts, principles, procedures, regulations, institutional arrangements, various participants who support public accountability, a range of legislation, the key ones being, uh, the Public Service Act, the Public Finance Act, and Crown Entities Act.

Uh, a number of agencies have monitoring roles and, uh, in particular, uh, the commission and the treasury. Um, and that's all part of this, this wider accountability system turned into the role of the Public Service Commissioner. Our functions is set out in the Public Service Act 2020, sort of in five broad areas.

The first being, um, broader leadership of the public service to improve services and outcomes, including for Maori, promoting integrity, accountability, and transparency, uh, acting as the employer of the public, uh, service chief executives reviewing the design and operation of the system of agencies, so machinery or government, as well as oversight of the public service, workforce and employment relations.

Um, each of those have an area of performance and accountability. Um, and importantly, we set standards and expectations of chief executives and the agencies, and we support them to achieve those expectations and then hold them to account, um, when they do not in the workforce, uh, area.

For example, all chief executives have an enduring expectation to develop and maintain the capability of the, the workforce, um, to engage with Maori and understand Maori perspectives. And that's important because that was codified in the Public Service Act for the very first time in 2020 under Section 40, which also, uh, for the first time emphasizes the important role the public service has in supporting the Crown and its relationships with Maori under the treaty.

So that's very explicit and upfront in our, in our act, uh, and that, so, so the capability of our workforce is an important thing within the control of the public service, and that's just really important. I wanna talk about some of the things we've done in that space. Also important, of course, because in order to improve outcomes, you need to understand what's expected, um, by Maori and for Maori, uh, in recent years.

So since the act, um, we've had quite a deliberate focus across the whole, the public service and lifting that capability. Um, and probably for the very first time have done that in a systemic way, in a structured way where all agencies have been working together trying to lift that. Whereas in the past, it was a bit more kind of ad hoc and up to individual agencies to do their thing and sort of optional, really.

Uh, so we've worked very closely at the commission with, of course, have got a lead role here on a range of initiatives. Um, and that includes trying to attract more Maori into the workforce, the public service workforce, and then up into leadership roles. So now all agencies do have a, a, a clear plan in place for building this capability, um, based around framework, and they're required to report progress in the annual report.

So that's a in important accountability mechanism. We've indu introduced new, um, Maori crown modules into a common core leadership development across the public service. Uh, and through quite targeted and concerted action, we've seen significant improvements in the number of proportion of Maori in our workforce, uh, including the senior leadership roles.

So recent workforce data shows the number of, uh, Maori leaders in the public services doubled in the last five years. And about 16.7% of public servant are Maori. So that's just higher than the, the Maori, um, uh, national, uh, workforce numbers. Uh, and in the area of pay and equity, um, we've more than half the gap in pay between Maori and non Maori and the public service.

In 2018, it was 11.2%, and now it sits at 4.8%. So that's quite a dramatic shift. So quite a deliberate focus structured approach to achieving that, that shift in our workforce capability, uh, in our integrity, um, role. We've got a leadership role there, obviously uphold the principles of the public service that to facilitate transparency and accountability, for example, we promote open government, we provide guidance and, and, um, advice on integrity matters.

And we set standards for conduct and behavior. Uh, and that is really closely linked to performance and effectiveness of public service agencies, which we rely on public trust and confidence in a high trust environment within and between agencies as key for our success. So we measure and we report trust and confidence in the public service every quarter.

Um, and our results show that New Zealand public service is one amongst the most trusted in the world ranking forth out of 30 OECD countries. Um, as of September this year, 79% of New Zealanders trust public services based on their personal experience and trust in the public service brand is 59%, but we know that for Maori, those, um, trust levels are much lower.

Do you have that data? Do you have those? I don't have the specific numbers here, but there is a strong correlation between the Maori demographic, but also social demographic graphics. But this comes to Dave's earlier point about looking at different models so that those who are most trusted in Maori communities are there in their time of need.

Mm-Hmm. And in, we have more recent experience that shows that public service is not always the best place to do that. And so it's a really important, um, point to make, not to jump in there. Approximately one and two Maori do not have trust in your public service. And, um, and I, I'll just, um, add to that, um, that's not necessarily because of the current arrangements and the current public servants that has to do with 184 years of history, I suggest, um, and it was something that was identified way back in 86 with John Maggie host report.

Mm, thank you, Dave. Um, I just wanna talk a little bit about outcomes. Uh, and this is just sort a really important point. It's the role of ministers and cabinet, ultimately the government to determine the outcomes they wish to prioritize. Public servants can't do that unilaterally, um, because that the government determines, uh, which resources are then allocated to those outcomes and how they wish to achieve those outcomes.

Public service supports the government and the delivery of those priorities with the resources allocated to them, how those outcomes are defined as quite critical as well. Um, and a lot of research shows that clear and measurable outcomes can lead to improved performance towards them.

So how well it's defined, is it actionable? That that's really important. Um, we can point to various sources. We've got some, um, there's a book that was produced by some staff from the commission, which looked at this targeting commitment, and that does have some good examples of, uh, government initiatives that have, uh, specifically measurable aims that then translate into practical actions, which lead to the delivery of improved outcomes.

Some of the prototypes that Dave was talking about, amongst those examples, key point where there's greater clarity of the outcomes being prioritized, it's easier to hold people to account or delivery towards them, especially when you can accurately measure and report on them. Thank you, Heather. Um, the last bit I'll just briefly mention is one of the key roles that we have at the commission is recruiting, um, the chief executives.

It's quite a rigorous process that we go through. They're appointed for a fixed term, usually about five years. We, um, set the performance expectations annually. We review performance against them. Uh, we have regular, um, engagement throughout the year with chief executives check in on their progress against the things that they're accountable for.

Uh, and importantly, we seek ministerial input into the setting of those expectations and their, um, the, uh, view on the performance of the chief executives and delivering on those government priorities and outcomes. I'll leave it there because I'll let Stacy speak to, um, her areas and allow a bit more time for questions from the committee. Thank you, Heather.

Thank you. Um, Cole, Stacey Wyman got not only, um, um, hi, I'm Stacy Weimer. Um, I'm the budget director at the, uh, treasury. Um, thank you very much for having me here today. Um, really keen to, to discuss this with you. Um, I thought I'd just start off by covering off Treasury's role in public, um, accountability settings in kind of two parts.

One is our stewardship role, uh, within the public finance system, and just how well or not, um, that public finance system delivers against its goals of kind of accountability and transparency. Those two things really go together. Um, and then the second bit, um, if it's of interest, uh, but I'll, I'll take your lead on that, is I can just talk about how we allocate resources through the budget process, um, and how that works to support accountability for outcomes.

So I'll just really briefly touch on that piece in particular, and, and then I can come back if there's any questions. So perhaps if I start with the public finance system, I mean, any sense, the public finance system is all about how do we collect revenue and then, um, uh, both the allocation and expenditure of public amount money and how we account for that.

Um, the core legislative, um, underpinnings of that are within the Public Finance Act. Um, but the scope of the public finance system is much broader than just the act. So there's a whole heap of kind of institutional norms and processes that work alongside it. Um, two really key roles that the public finance system and the Public Finance Act plays in relation to our discussion today is one around parliamentary authorization.

So that provides a framework for authorizing expenditure of public money. And then the second one is a concept of public accountability, and that's requiring the executive to, um, both articulate what it intends to achieve with, um, public resources, and then, uh, provide an account for what has been achieved as a result.

So, you know, our role in that system is, is about how do we ensure that, um, uh, accountability for outcomes, whether that's for Maori, whether that's for climate change, uh, and anything. So that kind of very much at that system, uh, at that system level. Um, in terms of transparency and accountability for articulating those, these kind of three key documents, um, there is, uh, each agency's strategic contentions, um, which, um, or commonly known as a statement of intent, which should set out, you know, very clearly what's the vision

and direction, um, what are we actually trying to achieve, what's our performance measures for, um, getting there, um, and how well are we doing against that or not. Uh, the second one is, um, the estimates. So that's part of the budget documentation. And again, that sets out both how the tax money, um, payer money is being allocated and spent in the performance measures upon which that judge, um, you know, uh, that performance should be judged.

Um, and then the third one is the annual report. You know, each agency's annual reports. Um, I'll just talk a little bit about how well that's working or not. Um, so an overall level, our public finance system is strong and it's well regarded internationally, but one of the biggest challenges that we face is actually in relation to that public accountability and in particular, improving performance reporting, whether that's, uh, for Maori outcomes, whether that's for, for any kind, any kind of outcomes.

And there's really two elements to that challenge. Um, we are really good at saying how much we have spent. So what is the dollars that we have spent on something, you know, um, um, as Dave talked about earlier, but we're, um, but clearly articulating, you know, what are the outcomes we are looking to achieve and how we'll measure our performance, um, against those outcomes is much more challenging.

And at that level, what you often get is not a lot of specificity. It comes back to what Heather was saying, you know, you just get kind of quite generalized statements. So it's actually really hard to see if those outcomes are being achieved and with the money that you're spending towards them is actually delivering those outcomes. So, so one is just about, you know, what we're actually, uh, measuring there.

The other one is actually tracing the money through. And, and by that I mean through those, um, documents that provide that transparency, particularly the estimates. So, um, you know, actually going right, what budget decisions have been made, um, through to how much money has been spent, and then what is the contribution to outcomes.

And the documents do not make it easy to actually follow that flow through from money spent through to outcomes being delivered. Couple of other points I'd make on that is, um, the solution is not more and more reporting. Um, you know, because then you just get into this overload of reporting. And so actually being, uh, really clear about, okay, what are the things we want to report on?

Quite a lot of this, as Heather talks about, you know, is down to government and their priorities, but, you know, what are the things that we want to report on? Um, and, and how do we be really clear about are they actually, is a money being spent actually contributing to those outcomes? Mm-Hmm. Um, these messages aren't new. Um, the, um, um, OAG and the parliamentary commissioner for the environment has also made, um, similar points as well.

So that's kind of in our, our system role. And, um, uh, and then in relation to budget, if it's okay for me just to talk for a few minutes longer, if that's okay. I guess what we're trying to do there is encourage people thinking about, um, the outcomes that they're seeking to, to deliver through the money that they are, um, bidding for in effect.

Um, and so we do that in a couple of ways. One is, um, in relation to the budget templates that agencies have to have to complete. Um, they require agencies to think about what are the broader income, um, outcomes. Um, they also look for, um, these questions relating to Treaty of Wang implications as well.

Um, and, uh, and more importantly also just about distributional impacts, you know, what is the impact of this initiative, um, uh, intended consequences, but what are the potential unintended consequences? So just thinking more broadly than, you know, this is a good initiative because of this. What are the other distributional impacts it might have? So that's from the, um, what we ask agencies to submit to us.

Um, then treasury does its own assessment, um, which is focused around, um, value for money that is not just about, um, kind of, uh, specific fiscal costs and benefits. We also assess what are the, um, distributional impacts, um, can this actually be delivered? Does it align with the government priorities as well?

Um, so that's kind of, I can go into that in more detail, but, uh, but, um, uh, that's kind of what we do in the budget space. Um, I guess just more broadly, I'm not sure if this is useful. Now, I can talk about what I think would be, actually no, I'll, sorry. I will stop there and, um, I'm just conscious of time, so I'll stop. Yeah. Um, thank you very much. Yeah.

Unfortunately we we're a bit tight for time, but, um, I'm looking around for questions. I, I might actually start committee if it's all right. So, um, take your, you know, there's a quote. Um, you can only, um, manage what you can measure, right? And, um, I take your point, Heather, about, it's also many ministers that are accountable, but ministers come and go.

Governments come and go. Your agencies are still here. And, um, you know, I think, uh, there's a collective acknowledgement that Maori outcomes, uh, really haven't improved over many governments. So I'm wanting to understand what kind of reporting and data gathering are you doing to understand how are your agencies, um, monitoring other agencies and, and, um, uh, uh, giving the most important data to the ministers to understand, for example, for public service commission, how many CEOs are Maori across our public service?

How many leadership teams are Maori? You know, what percentage of Maori, um, are in our leadership service for, um, treasury? It's like, well, um, how much are we spending to improve Maori education performance? And it actually, is it making a difference? So I, I guess I'm really keen to understand what level of, um, data maturity you, your organizations are keeping to understand, uh, how we actually delivering for Maori across time.

Shall I just Briefly speak to the workforce? Um, data, this is something that we've worked on proving over the years. Used to be an annual production of, um, data on our, and we put it out, uh, publicly. We now do that on a, on a quarterly basis. And we have, um, have had much more of a specific focus on diversity, but including for Maori representation.

Um, but it's not just in the data, it's using that data to inform us about where we need to make some changes and where we need to target. Even though we have increased the number of Maori leadership roles, um, uh, uh, and in the workforce, there are some professions within the public service. Like for example, in the policy profession, we've still got some way to go to ensure Maori perspectives and voices are in that profession as well.

Uh, the other, um, way that we collect data, uh, is through what's called a public service census. We did this for the very first time in New Zealand, back in 2021. Now the jurisdictions do this on a regular basis. We are going to run this again next year, and that's where we, um, get information about things like, um, uh, how well, uh, the public service understands their treaty responsibilities, how well supported they are to learn to deal Maori and grow their capability.

Um, our first lot of results were looking fairly positive, um, about 73% of, um, public servants, um, value to deal their Del Maori and wish to grow it. And 65% feel supported in doing so. Expecting to see that shift next year, but we'll wait.

And soon we run, run that survey, um, text again. So that's, That's our in the workforce. Yep. Um, so I think, uh, one of the areas which definitely needs improvement is evaluation of, um, of policy, um, uh, policy initiative. So it's an area where, um, and this is across the board that actually there is not sufficient evaluation done of whether particular policy, um, initiatives, um, are actually working or not.

So, um, uh, that's probably, uh, you know, that does create limitations across the board. Um, in terms of that, just, um, you know, treasury has a second opinion role, so we will typically comment on, um, other people's or other agencies. So when agencies are putting either proposals for forward, uh, forward, either through the budget or, um, through cabinet papers more generally, um, treasury will comment on those.

Um, uh, uh, so, you know, just to your question about what data do we hold, actually, we rely on the agencies quite a bit from a, uh, from a specific data where it's not fiscal data. So obviously we have a lot of fiscal information, but we are reliant more on agencies in relation to specific, um, data relating to their particular area.

The other thing I just mentioned here, which is incredibly powerful, is the IDI, the, um, integrated data infrastructure, um, which, um, we have used, um, actually as an example when we were looking at a, um, uh, redeveloping the state housing in, um, um, and that is incredibly powerful to go, well, actually, what are the outcomes for different, um, people?

Um, it's all anonymized, but it is at a, um, at a mesh block level, so at a very low level. So you can look at, you know, what are the outcomes for different people depending upon the circumstances they are in. Can I just pick up on your Yeah. Yes. Uh, suck on that. Just a supplementing on that. Uh, and it's, um, around the same topic, but I wanted to use the example that Dave gave, um, that, you know, if you've got three different agencies all putting resource into one COPA and then you have a result at the end, how is that all?

Is there a place to go that measures the overall outcome from, you know, input from three different organizations? Um, I, I think the, uh, answer to that outside of prototypes, like the one that I've just mentioned, which is a small innovation, um, at a government wide level, I point to the social investment agency and social investment now, and that's because, and your question, Dan, was about monitoring.

That's because in the public service now of that $70 billion we spend, we can't prove attribution for any program. That's not just the Maori programs, that's any program that, so if you mon, if you continue to monitor and focusing on monitoring the way that we have been doing it, we will continue to get the same result, which is, we are underperforming, but with the inability to prove attribution, you can't identify what works.

So monitoring is half of the, the issue, and we have done this for decades monitoring, but we've never looked at what works. And the, the key to this are in those, you know, drawing strengths from our past and looking at what has happened in the past. And it's the same thing. It's reoccurring attribution is key in terms of, uh, how do you prove that attribution, it's a combination of standards, guidance, and the IDI and that is where social investment is going.

And I suspect that of that 15%, um, of the population where we spend half of our social sector spend 35 billion, that's where that will come into play. I think that's really key. Moving from innovation to a system wide change monitoring is important, but if I know anything from Ali's experience over the last decades is monitoring will only tell you what's not working.

Mm-Hmm. And no matter what I do with public, what we do with public servants, and, you know, the, the work that Heather has talked about is important, but that will not shift the dial on its own because of trust and confidence. Mm-Hmm. Because they just will not come. We saw that in Covid, we saw that in, in, um, the prototype for tu we saw that in house.

We need to move this at a system level Yeah. And social investment. To answer your question, I think that's where I would put my focus. Yeah. Uh, we've got a new question from Raymond, If that's okay with our position members. Okay. Look, um, thank you everyone. Uh, please. Um, please don't take anything personal that I'm about to ask.

But, but I think from learning about how, how deep and detailed, and actually if I, if you allow me to say this word, how powerful the role is of, of treasury and, and, and PSC, I'm just wondering, is there, you know, is there too much on TPK to achieve these outcomes when these, your two agencies can play a much more stronger role in helping us in this COPA seeing, um, outcomes for Maori?

Um, you know, I'm, I'm just wondering, and correct me if I'm wrong, but I'm getting the sense that there's so much pressure and responsibility on TPK, but I'm learning that you guys actually have a lot that you can do as well. Um, any thoughts about that reflection? Um, do you wanna start off? There you Go. And respectfully Yeah, I was gonna say, you know, our role, um, particularly Tru you's role and as I indicated is at that systems level.

So what are the system levers? And they talked about that as well, because actually if you get, for example, if you get better evaluation across the board, that also helps in relation to mari outcomes to understand what is working and what isn't. And so our role is to try and provide that standard and guidance. And yet there are certainly some areas that we get more involved in, uh, than others, uh, for example.

But that's, you know, when I think about that's our role there and it's, it's about, um, how do we, um, support agencies to deliver as they should be delivering as well. So Yeah. But it's that system, it's that system kind of what are your system levers? It's, it is a fair question. It is a fair question and one that we ask ourselves often.

Um, and it is an area, particularly under this government's, um, setting of the nine targets where there's quite specific measurable things that the look to change in terms of outcomes, education for achievement, uh, and attendance. Um, it's forcing us as central agencies, including with DPMC, to work more closely together to, to join up our view and advice on how we're supporting one, the development those lead agencies and the agencies involved, how they are preparing their delivery plans, um, providing assurance up to ministers

and to those agencies on what, what, whether we think they're adequate, uh, the monitoring and reporting of progress against, um, the, those, the progress on those targets. And it's flowing through a number of the sort of mechanisms that we have, um, in our gift essential agencies, for example, in our area, reflecting that through to the chief executive performance expectations, and then also exploring new tools like performance pay that's strongly linked to those targets.

So shifting our, our approach as agencies, central agencies to how do we, um, mobilize around these very clear targets set by government. So if you, if I can just ask supplementary and, and I'm just linking something you said earlier, Heather, you said about that survey that was done in 2021 Yeah. And it's going to be rolled out soon.

Um, I, I was, I was struck by when you said about what you ask your, um, the, the public servants. I'm wondering, is there a question about do you know how big the problem is, as in the people working for us? Do you know how big the problem is and how important it is to reach outcomes in this area?

Hmm. So in terms of the chief executive performance expectations, so, um, they're set by the commissioner. The commissioner is responsible for the performance of the, of the ces. We do this annually. Um, each year we sort of refine and improve sort of what we cover, um, broadly speaking, government priorities, how well they run the agency, um, uh, and then their, their contribution to the system.

So how are they advancing some of these system enduring commitments like stewardship, um, building Maori crown capability in terms of the, um, government priorities, we, um, consult with ministers to understand exactly what it is that they're expecting of the chief executive to deliver. Uh, sometime it's really clear, like the Prime Minister writes a letter to a minister and says, your portfolio priorities are, these will reflect those directly into the performance expectations.

Uh, we've worked through a process with the chief executive to confirm and agree that those are the priorities for them for the year ahead. Um, in some cases there might be a review or some other report that specifically identifies things within the agencies that they're required to prioritize. Um, we have performance improvement reviews.

So that's a, um, a process where we have independent people who come in and look at the performance of an agency with a view to their sort of four year performance horizon that might have recommendations about the things that agency needs to work on to improve their performance. We reflect that into the performance expectations. Um, we don't, um, publicize the content because they are private information between the commission and their employees.

Uh, the framework of that, however, I think is available, um, sort of broadly what the, the components of those performance expectations. Uh, we have a end of year formal review, but throughout the year we're checking in on progress.
